problem_statement: A municipal permit portal began returning HTTP 422 for a narrow class of address-bearing PDF exports after a routine release. Most exports still succeed, and the release also refreshed a certificate bundle and enabled a validation flag that had already been enabled in staging. Determine the earliest causal break and whether a root-cause change is justified from the supplied evidence.

observations.statement: Production telemetry for release r71 shows 422 responses on 91 of 94 sanitized requests whose address contains an explicitly empty unit field; 4,812 requests without that shape completed normally.
An isolated replay of captured request apt-17 returns 200 on release r70 and 422 on r71 with the request bytes, rendered settings, dependency lock, and container base digest held constant.

The release diff moves removal of empty optional address fields ahead of schema validation. The validator still requires the unit key whenever address_type is apartment, so the changed control flow presents an incomplete object to the unchanged validator.

In a focused isolated test, applying only the normalization hunk to r70 reproduces the 422; reverting only that hunk on r71 restores 200. Input, settings, dependency wheels, and certificate bundle remain byte-identical in both arms.
The certificate bundle changed in the same release, but TLS handshakes complete before every sampled 422 and both old and new bundles produce the same application response in isolation.
STRICT_ADDRESS_VALIDATION is true in production and staging. Toggling only that setting changes error wording but does not make apt-17 succeed on r71.
Clearing the export deduplication cache and issuing a new request identifier leaves the 422 unchanged, while cache hit rate and queue depth remain within their normal bands.
A second availability zone running the same r71 image exhibits the same request-shape failure despite separate nodes, process lifetimes, and load balancers.
